# VLM-Based OCR: Evaluating New Approaches

## Overview

Vision Language Models (VLMs) offer a fundamentally different approach to OCR compared to traditional methods. Rather than detecting individual characters and assembling them into words, VLMs "read" a document image holistically — much as a human would — and produce structured text output.

For GLAM institutions sitting on large digitised collections with ageing OCR, this raises a practical question: **is it worth re-processing, and how do you decide?**

This chapter won't recommend a specific model — the landscape moves too fast for that. Instead, it gives you a framework for evaluating VLM-based OCR on *your* collections, and shares lessons learned from running these evaluations at scale.

## The Core Insight: There Is No Best OCR Model

The single most important finding from our benchmarking work is that **document type determines which model you should use**. A small, lightweight model can outperform one four times its size on one collection, and the rankings flip completely on another.

This is counterintuitive if you're used to thinking about OCR as a solved problem with one "best" tool. In practice:

- Models trained heavily on modern documents often struggle with historical printed text.
- Models that excel on clean printed pages may fail on handwritten card catalogues.
- The highest-scoring model on a general benchmark may rank last on your specific materials.

::: {.callout-important}
## Don't Trust General Benchmarks Alone
Published benchmark scores tell you how a model performs on *that benchmark's* documents. Your collections are different. Always evaluate on a representative sample of your own materials before committing to a model.
:::

## What VLMs Change

### Output Format

Traditional OCR pipelines (Tesseract, ABBYY) typically produce ALTO XML or hOCR — formats that preserve exact character coordinates on the page. This is useful for highlighting search results on a page image, but the output is complex to work with and carries no semantic understanding of the document.

VLM-based OCR typically outputs **Markdown** — a human-readable format that preserves document structure (headings, lists, tables) semantically rather than spatially. This is a meaningful trade-off:

| | Traditional OCR (ALTO/hOCR) | VLM OCR (Markdown) |
|---|---|---|
| **Coordinates** | Per-character bounding boxes | None (unless explicitly requested) |
| **Structure** | Physical layout | Semantic structure |
| **Downstream use** | Page-image overlay, display | LLM integration, RAG, search |
| **Complexity** | High (XML parsing) | Low (plain text) |

Neither format is strictly better — it depends on what you need the text for. If your primary use case is full-text search, RAG, or feeding text into other AI systems, Markdown output is significantly easier to work with.

### New Capabilities

Beyond plain text extraction, VLMs can:

- **Handle mixed content** — handwriting alongside print, stamps, annotations
- **Understand layout semantically** — distinguish headers from body text, identify tables, recognise captions
- **Extract structured data directly** — with the right prompting, a VLM can output JSON rather than plain text (see the [Structured Information Extraction](patterns/structured-generation/vlm-structured-generation.qmd) chapter)
- **Work across languages** without switching engines

### New Failure Modes

VLMs also introduce failure modes that traditional OCR doesn't have:

- **Hallucination**: When faced with degraded or illegible text, a VLM may *invent* plausible-sounding content rather than producing garbled characters. This is arguably more dangerous than traditional OCR errors, which are obviously wrong.
- **Illustration confusion**: Pages with images, maps, or diagrams can cause VLMs to describe what they see rather than transcribe text, or to hallucinate text that isn't there.
- **Repetition loops**: Certain layouts (particularly maps with repeated labels) can trigger the model to produce the same phrase hundreds of times.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Hallucination Is the Key Risk
Traditional OCR fails visibly — garbled characters are obviously wrong. VLM hallucinations can look perfectly plausible. Any VLM-based OCR pipeline needs quality checks that account for this.
:::

## Evaluating OCR for Your Collections

### The Pairwise Comparison Approach

Rather than trying to measure absolute accuracy (which requires expensive ground-truth transcriptions), a practical approach is **pairwise comparison**: show a judge two OCR outputs for the same page and ask which is better.

The judge can be:

- **A human reviewer** — gold standard but slow and expensive
- **A large language model** — fast and cheap, with surprisingly good agreement with human judgements when the model can see the original document image
- **A combination** — use LLM judgement at scale, validate with a small sample of human comparisons

This produces a **relative ranking** of models on your specific documents, which is exactly what you need for a procurement or migration decision.

### What to Include in Your Evaluation Sample

Choose a sample that represents the diversity of your collection:

- **Clean, well-printed pages** — the easy case; most models handle this well
- **Degraded or foxed pages** — tests robustness to noise
- **Complex layouts** — multi-column text, tables, marginalia
- **Handwritten content** — if present in your collections
- **Non-text pages** — illustrations, maps, blank pages — tests whether the model gracefully handles pages with little or no text
- **Mixed-language pages** — if applicable

50–150 pages is typically enough to produce a reliable ranking. The key is diversity, not volume.

### Cheap Signals for Quality

Before running expensive evaluations, there are simple heuristics that can flag problematic OCR output at scale with zero model inference:

- **Text length**: Abnormally short or long output for a given page size
- **Alphabetic character ratio**: Low ratio suggests garbled output
- **Repetition detection**: Repeated phrases or character sequences
- **Character entropy**: Very low entropy suggests repetitive output; very high suggests noise

These signals won't tell you *which* model is best, but they can quickly identify pages where any model has failed — useful for quality assurance on large batch runs.

## Cost and Scale

VLM-based OCR has become remarkably affordable. As a rough guide, processing hundreds of thousands of pages on rented GPU infrastructure now costs tens of dollars rather than thousands — a fraction of what the original digitisation cost.

The key cost factors are:

- **Model size**: Smaller models (under 2B parameters) can run on cheaper GPUs and process pages faster
- **GPU type**: Consumer-grade GPUs work for small models; larger models need more VRAM
- **Batch size**: Processing pages in batches rather than one-at-a-time dramatically improves throughput
- **Error rate**: A model that processes 99% of pages correctly but fails on 1% may cost more in manual review than a slightly slower model with fewer failures

::: {.callout-tip}
## Start Small, Scale Gradually
Run your evaluation on 50–150 pages first. Once you've identified the best model for your documents, do a larger pilot (a few thousand pages) before committing to a full collection run. This catches edge cases that small samples miss.
:::

## When to Consider Re-OCRing

Re-processing an existing collection with VLM-based OCR makes sense when:

1. **Your existing OCR is poor quality** — particularly common with collections digitised more than 10 years ago, or with complex/historical materials
2. **You need the text for AI applications** — RAG, semantic search, or training data all benefit from cleaner, better-structured text
3. **The cost is justified** — for large collections, even cheap per-page costs add up; weigh against the value of improved text
4. **You have a quality assurance plan** — VLM hallucination means you need some way to validate output, even if it's just the cheap heuristic signals described above

Re-processing does *not* mean discarding your existing OCR. Keep ALTO/hOCR for coordinate-based applications and add VLM output as an additional text layer.

## Tools

The [`ocr-bench`](https://github.com/davanstrien/ocr-bench) tool implements the evaluation approach described in this chapter — pairwise VLM-as-judge comparisons with ELO rankings, human validation, and per-collection leaderboards. It is designed for GLAM collections and can be customised for different document types.

## Practical Steps

1. **Assemble a representative sample** — 50–150 pages covering the diversity of your collection
2. **Run 3–4 candidate models** — choose models of different sizes and architectures
3. **Compare outputs pairwise** — using LLM-as-judge with human validation on a subset
4. **Check for failure modes** — specifically test on difficult pages (illustrations, degraded text, blank pages)
5. **Cost a full run** — extrapolate from your pilot timing and GPU costs
6. **Run a larger pilot** — a few thousand pages to catch edge cases
7. **Implement quality signals** — automated checks on the full output before human review
